data.title: Bank7 Should Have Jumped After A Strong Q3
data.description:
Bank7 overview

October 15th was a fantastic day for shareholders of Bank7 (NASDAQ:BSVN). Even though the stock barely budged, inching up 0.6%, fundamental data for the third quarter of its 2025 fiscal year came in stronger than expected. Management seems optimistic, with the firm targeting meaningful loan growth this year. And on top of this, the enterprise has been able to retain high asset quality and a share price that, relative to earnings, is attractive. Although I would have liked to have seen profitability improve year over year, that alone is not a deal breaker. Given the totality of the circumstances, I have no issue keeping the company as a 'buy' candidate, which is what I have had it rated since I upgraded it back in July of this year. And the great thing is that the stock is even cheaper than it was back then, with shares currently down 6.1%. This offers investors a good opportunity to jump in on the cheap.

A great quarter… mostly

The 2025 fiscal year might be shaping up to be a bit worse for Bank7 relative to what the company achieved in 2024. But compared to what analysts have been anticipating, things are looking really good. Before we get into the headline items, I think digging deeper and talking about the firm's balance sheet would be a great idea. During the quarter, the company booked $1.64 billion of deposits. That was up from the $1.59 billion it had in the second quarter of this year. And it translated to a nice jump over the $1.52 billion the company ended last year with.

I am always concerned about risk in the banking sector. I have historically viewed this space as riskier than most other spaces. Companies that are in seemingly good shape can quickly collapse, and they can do so for a variety of reasons. One of these is excessive exposure to uninsured deposits. In looking for attractive opportunities, I prefer those with uninsured deposit exposure of 30% or less. And sure enough, Bank7 meets that, with $401.8 million, or 24.6%, of its deposits classified as uninsured. And if we adjust for certain items, such as insider and collateralized deposits, this drops even further to $309.6 million, or 18.9% of the total. This is certainly promising to me.

Loans are every bit as important as deposits. At the end of last year, the company had $1.38 billion of capital tied up in these. This year, by the third quarter, they had increased to $1.51 billion. The greatest exposure that the institution has here would be to the 26.5% of its loan portfolio that involves commercial and industrial loans. But outside of that, the institution is incredibly diverse, with the next largest exposure being the 18.9% dedicated to hospitality properties in the commercial real estate category. Other major categories include non-owner-occupied real estate, one to four family commercial real estate, and owner occupied commercial real estate. There's also a good amount of commercial construction loans on its books.

This does bring me to one of the risk factors that I think are important to take into consideration though. This would be the company's exposure to variable loans. We are now back in a declining interest rate environment. And about 79% of its loan portfolio is variable rate in nature. Management actually describes the enterprise as being well positioned for a declining rate environment. In fact, the business illustrated, in the image below, different floors on these variable rates. A lot of its overall loan portfolio is variable, but it is variable subject to a floor, which would mean that no matter how much interest rates decline, the picture would be fine in the near term. And the good news is that, as interest rates drop, much of its deposits have no floor at all. This means that the company can reduce how much it is paying to depositors while still capturing higher yields like what it has now.

But longer term, the situation is a little different. $574.6 million, or approximately 38.3%, of its gross loans in the second quarter of this year matured in one year or less. We don't actually have data for the most recent quarter yet since management has not provided their official quarterly report. But that number is probably not much different today. And then, you have another $850.1 million, or 56.7%, of its loans that mature between one year out and five years out. All combined in the second quarter, this amounted to about 95% of loans. So even though the company will be able to capture some benefit in the short run, over the next couple of years now, we can expect a convergence between what it's able to capture on its loans and whatever interest rates drop down to. But honestly, a lot of banks are like that. So I'm not terribly surprised.

The value of securities came in at $54.9 million for the most recent quarter. That compared to the $59.9 million the institution had at the end of last year. Cash and cash equivalents over this time went from $240.9 million to $256.7 million. In addition to this, the company saw debt remained at $0, as it has for a while now. This is always great to see. And it signifies that the overall risk profile, is especially when you consider uninsured deposit exposure, is low.

Net interest income for the company totaled $22.3 million for the quarter. That was up from the $21.2 million reported the previous year. This rise came about in part because the firm's net interest margin inched up from 5.02% to 5.07%. But honestly, the heavy lifting was the result of the increase in the size of the company's balance sheet. Things would have been even better had it not been for a $0.7 million provision for credit losses that the company experienced in the third quarter of this year. That same weakness dulled returns for the first nine months of this year, resulting in net interest income inching up only slightly from $64.5 million last year to $64.9 million this year.

The problem child ended up being non-interest income. This actually contracted from $3.7 million in the third quarter of 2024 to $2.2 million the same time this year. This was because of a drop in 'other' income. And for the first nine months of this year, that same category pushed non-interest income down from $8.9 million to $6.7 million. This is probably a good time to talk about how the company fared compared to expectations. Actual net revenue for the institution came in at $25.2 million for the most recent quarter. That was up from the $24.9 million reported the previous year and beat analysts' expectations by $0.9 million.

Unfortunately, this did not help the bottom line all that much. Because of rising costs, such as salary and employee benefits costs, as well as other miscellaneous expenses, net income actually dipped year over year from $11.8 million to $10.8 million. That caused earnings per share to contract from $1.24 to $1.13. However, this reading did exceed what analysts were expecting to the tune of $0.07 per share. And for the first nine months of this year, the $32.3 million in net income achieved by the company was only a bit worse than the $34.6 million reported a year earlier.

BSVN shares are decently priced & asset quality is strong

There are a couple of different ways that I value financial institutions. One of these is in relation to earnings. I strongly prefer banks that are trading at a price to earnings multiple of 10 or lower. And sure enough, if we project out results based on what we have seen already this year, we would get exactly the high end of that range. The chart above illustrates that this also puts the company below four of the five enterprises that I decided to compare it to. In the chart below, I did the same type of analysis using both the price to book and price to tangible book multiples. In this case, the picture was flipped, with Bank7 trading at a premium to these other players.

This would typically justify a more neutral assessment. But there is something that makes up for this in my view. This is the fact that asset quality for the institution is top notch. In the first chart below, you can see its return on assets reading of 2.33%. This is significantly above the 1% minimum that I like to see. And it also puts the company well above any of the five firms that I compared it to. In the chart below that, I did the same type of analysis using return on equity. With a reading of 18.11%, Bank7 is leagues above the 10% minimum that I want to see. And, once again, the other players don't even come close.

Credit quality is also a big contributor here. As a rule of thumb, I like to see 0.50% or less of total assets that are non-performing. But according to management, this figure amounted to only 0.28% in the third quarter of this year. This actually marked an improvement over the 0.43% reported for the end of 2024 and it was meaningfully below the 1.64% reading that we get for 2023. As the image below illustrates, other vital credit quality metrics have gotten better as well. This shows that the institution is getting better as opposed to worse. And with a strong pipeline under its belt, with the firm targeting a high single digit year over year growth rate in loans, I have no reason to be anything other than optimistic. Of course, the quality and value of these loans will matter. Management did warn that recent loans have come in at between the 7% and 7.25% range compared to their previous hope of a 7.4% figure. That, combined with competitive pressures when it comes to deposits, should be monitored.

Takeaway

Fundamentally, I must say that I am a fan of what I see with Bank7. The institution is doing a fine job and its future looks bright to me. Shares might not be the cheapest relative to book value and tangible book value. But on a price to earnings basis, the company is certainly appealing. Add on top of this it's robust asset quality and attractive credit quality metrics, and I must say that I am still bullish enough to keep it rated a 'buy' even though there are certain areas that investors should watch such as interest rate exposure.

data.title: Fiduciary Management Q3 2025 Investment Strategy Outlook
data.description:
Stock markets raced higher in the third quarter, with the Russell 2000, S&P 500, MSCI EAFE (Local/USD), and MSCI World Indices gaining 12.39%, 8.12%, 5.38%/4.77%, and 7.27% respectively. The steep advance was fueled by a September rate cut by the Federal Reserve, unwavering enthusiasm around artificial intelligence (AI) and mega cap technology, and a frenzy of speculative buying activity. While it is impossible to know if we are officially in a stock market bubble, it certainly has many of the earmarks.

Fundamental Disconnect

While you would never know it when looking at how many global stock markets are trading at or near record highs, there are plenty of areas of weakness across the global economy. Global GDP growth is expected to fall to 2.9% in 2026 (from 3.2% in 2025), with the U.S., Europe, Japan, and China all slowing, according to the OECD. Labor markets are showing signs of easing, inflation remains elevated, and debt levels are substantial (with sizeable refinancing maturities on the horizon). Trade tensions remain high, with the full effect of tariff increases still yet to be felt.

In the U.S., the world’s largest economy, employment has slumped, the housing market is soft, construction spending is declining, ISM manufacturing activity has contracted in 32 out of the last 34 months, and transportation markets are in a “freight recession.” The low-income consumer has been under pressure and is cutting back, while the high-income consumer (a direct beneficiary of outsized stock market returns) is partying like it’s 1999.

In the past, consumer confidence and stock market confidence have generally moved in harmony. Today, consumer confidence is low (and falling) at a time when stock market confidence is soaring (see graph on the top right). This dichotomy speaks to the growing disconnect between Wall Street and Main Street, which is unlikely to persist over the long run.

Record Valuations

We are currently in one of the most expensive stock markets in U.S. history. The bottom right chart tracks eight traditional valuation metrics over the last 125 years, with the latest reading hitting the highest level on record, and that was before the market advanced again in September.

Looking different than the market is currently a disadvantage, but we can take some comfort knowing that small cap, international, and value stocks all trade at a sizeable discount to U.S. large cap growth. That said, rising tides have lifted most boats, and absolute valuations have moved higher across asset classes. Fortunately, as owners of quality businesses with strong balance sheets, FMI’s portfolios trade at a discount to their primary benchmarks.

Speculative Excess

Exuberance is abundant and complacency is high, both of which may continue. Goldman Sachs tracks the top-performing themes in the U.S. and Europe (table on the right). Bitcoin sensitive equities and European defense stocks are up over 100% this year, while meme stocks and China ADRs climb the leaderboard. Pockets of lower-quality businesses (commodities, European rate sensitive financials) have also outperformed. Notably, quality compounders and ex-tech compounders have been left behind.

Goldman Sachs also tracks various AI-themed baskets within the U.S., as shown below. The worst performing AI basket has more than doubled the year to date return of the S&P 500, with the best performing basket outpacing the index by nearly 5-fold.

Unprecedented Concentration

As displayed below, the market is narrow, with 10 holdings in the S&P 500 that are heavily influenced by AI reaching nearly 35% of the index. Prior stock market bubbles have been characterized by outsized sector performance, a lack of stock market breadth, and an unwavering adoration for a handful of market darlings, all of which are observed today.

As we wrote in our June 2023 letter, investors ought to consider an important question: are the winners of today going to be the winners of tomorrow? History would suggest no, as can be seen in the graphic below. Only one out of the top ten companies in the S&P 500 in 2005 is still in the top ten 20 years later. Historically, leaders change over time. Success invites competition and innovation can lead to disintermediation. Furthermore, despite what may appear to be strong competitive positioning and growth prospects, any company can be a bad investment if you overpay for it. In the 1970s, the “Nifty Fifty” were believed to be the best of the best; no price was too high for these businesses, and everyone piled in. In the 2000 tech bubble, many companies were also thought to be unassailable. Today, it appears these historical lessons have been forgotten.

A New Technology Paradigm (AI)

While AI has tremendous long-term potential and could certainly be a revolutionary technology, we marvel at the remarkable amount of capital expenditure (capex) being deployed. It reminds us of the fiber optic overbuild in the 2000 era, where massive amounts of capital were being spent on network infrastructure to take advantage of the growth of the internet. Simultaneous overbuilding by big competitors led to massive overcapacity that far outstripped demand. Many actors behaved irrationally, with debt exacerbating the problem. While it was true that the internet would ultimately change the world, vast amounts of capital were destroyed through malinvestment.

We are not naysayers on the prospects for generative AI. However, we are skeptical that the colossal amount of money being spent will generate attractive returns. As illustrated on the top right, a mega cap tech cohort of only five companies is on pace to spend nearly $400 billion in capex in the coming year. With each new AI capex announcement, investors get rewarded with an even bigger jump in market cap. McKinsey & Company does not see an end in sight, forecasting nearly $7 trillion more will be spent on datacenter capex by 2030. On the flip side, AI monetization remains an open question, with limited revenue generation thus far and an absence of “killer apps.” AI-native revenues (where AI is fundamental to a company’s product and operations) are believed to be less than $20 billion in total, paling in comparison to the amount of total investment. Ironically, increasing competition and capital intensity would tend to have an inverse relationship with stock market returns, yet investors are willing to look right through in the current environment. It remains to be seen if this dynamic is sustainable.

FOMO: Fear of Missing Out

The average investor underperforms the market by a wide margin over the long-term, as the DALBAR “Quantitative Analysis of Investor Behavior” studies have repeatedly shown. This can be attributed to predictable behavioral mistakes, namely piling into stocks after a period of strong performance (i.e. chasing returns), and aggressive selling when markets come under severe pressure; effectively doing the wrong things at the wrong times. Human emotion plays a crucial part in these behaviors, as the role of fear and greed cannot be underestimated.

Given the precarious backdrop we have today, we find it interesting (and predictable) that global equity ETFs added a record $152 billion of inflows over the past 3 weeks, as reported by EPFR and Bank of America. The technology sector gathered over $9.3 billion last week alone, the biggest weekly inflow on record. As the bottom right chart depicts, classic herd behavior is alive and well. U.S. households’ exposure to public equities have climbed to a new all-time high, well above prior stock market peaks.

-----

Years from now there will be a more definitive view on 2025 relative to history. In the meantime, we stay the course and observe the following: economic growth is showing cracks, valuations are stretched, excess is abundant, the market is concentrated, AI exuberance is unrelenting, and investors are piling into equities at a record pace. Our portfolios look very different than our benchmarks, with a focus on idiosyncratic dislocations. With an eye toward business quality, balance sheet strength, and valuation, we will continue to proceed with caution. Downside protection has been a hallmark at FMI over our 45-year history. If volatility increases significantly and we find ourselves in a bear market, we will be poised to take advantage of more opportunistic valuations.

Listed below are a few portfolio holdings that we find compelling over the long term:

OneMain Holdings Inc. (OMF) – Small Cap/All Cap

OneMain is a scale leader in nonprime personal lending. Although they have a messy corporate history, our research suggests that in most key areas they are superior to their peers. We believe with a few years of clean results and an improved macro environment, this company could be reframed as a differentiated, high-quality financial. Given organic growth opportunities in personal lending, and newer areas such as credit card and direct auto, we believe they can grow organically in the mid-single digits with operating leverage, while at the same time returning significant capital through a large dividend and episodic buybacks. Although the current macro environment has stressed their core consumer and pressured net charge offs, we believe the income statement alone provides sufficient protection from an adverse outcome in nearly all reasonable scenarios, with further protection on the balance sheet in a tail risk scenario. The macro backdrop has given us the opportunity to own this industry leading business at an attractive valuation.

Becton Dickinson & Co. (BDX) – Large Cap/All Cap/Global

Becton Dickinson is a medical supply and device company, selling low-cost medical essentials. Products include vascular access devices, pre-fillable syringes, and catheters, to name a few. Becton is the leader in most of its product lines and often competes in oligopoly market structures. Their management team has been executing on a strategy to grow and simplify the business. This has been successful but is obscured by noise in the financials caused by the pandemic, portfolio actions, and other temporary factors, providing us with an opportunity to buy a quality business at an attractive price. We believe execution on the company’s growth strategy and pending business separation will unlock value, with management targeting over 5% annual organic revenue growth and continued margin expansion in the coming years. Manufacturing scale is a key competitive advantage, with the company manufacturing billions of devices annually. Driven by healthcare utilization, the business is durable. Post their separation of biosciences and diagnostics solutions business in 2026, ~90% of revenue is recurring in nature (consumables). The valuation is attractive given the company’s defensive attributes and growth potential.

Greggs PLC (OTCPK:GGGSF)[GRG LN] – International/Global

Greggs is a vertically integrated food-on-the-go operator with over 2,650 shops across the UK. Greggs sells products which are low ticket, consumed immediately, and often repurchased during the day. The company’s strong brand and differentiated business model have created loyal customers and long-term market share gains. Elevated cost inflation and growth investments have coincided with a demand slowdown, weighing on the near-term outlook and pressuring the stock. While sales growth and margins are likely to take a step back in the interim, given investments being made across the business (supply chain, innovation, employees, channels/ partnership), we think the company has a long runway for new store openings. The company is cresting the peak of their supply chain investment cycle this year, management remains disciplined, and they are in an excellent financial position with cash flow set to inflect thereafter. We view the valuation as too low relative to their long-term prospects.

Thank you for your continued support of Fiduciary Management, Inc.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Week 241: $24,100 Allocated, $2,649.70 In Projected Dividends
data.description:
This week started off strong and ended with a bang as President Trump sent the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq down -2.71% and -3.56% on Friday, 10/10/25. President Trump stated that out of nowhere China had been sending letters to countries throughout the world indicating that they want to impose export controls on each and every element of production having to do with rare earths. After China responded President Trump followed up by stating that he would impose an additional 100% tariff on China and threatened to pull out of his scheduled meeting with President Xi Jinping in 2 weeks at the APEX in South Korea. Everything was going well, the Fed cut rates for the first time in 2025, and we were heading into an earning season that was expected to show EPS growth only to have a potential trade war rekindle. While the posturing between President Trump and President Xi Jinping could last significantly longer, I think the war of words today will ultimately be resolved and lead to an end-of-the-year rally. If the pullback continues into next week I won’t hesitate to make additional purchases because we have seen this story play out before and I don’t believe the United States and China will hold the global economy confined as it will become a no win situation for everyone. While this was a difficult week to stomach I believe that the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is structured to generate large amounts of recurring income that will be reinvested while mitigating downside risk as the latest spat unfolds.

After the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq snapped their losing streak last week, some unforeseen events took the market much lower on Friday. After allocating $24,100 to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, the account balance finished week 241 at $31,027.83. The account's profitability declined by -12.79% (-$1,015.63) to $6,927.83, which is a return on invested capital of 28.75%. This week I added a new position in the Adams Diversified Equity Fund (ADX) to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $36.68 of dividend income in week 241. The combination of reinvesting the generated dividend income and making these investments increased the forward projected annualized dividend income by $13.18 (0.50%) to $2,649.70. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has generated $5,001.99 of income since its inception, which is 20.84% of the total capital I have allocated toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. Obviously this wasn’t a great week by any means, but in my opinion the downturn was overdone. I believe a lot of stop losses were triggered, and many companies that have nothing to do with what is going on were impacted as well. I think that this will play out similar to the situation in April, and if the downturn is extended, it will be an opportunity for long-term investors. I am looking forward to allocating capital as the market retraces and believe that things will rebound throughout Q3 earnings.

The Overall Performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio

Since I am adding capital each week, I am focusing on how much my profitability moves rather than the return on invested capital. While the profitability took a hit of -12.79% the actual return on invested capital only fell -4.35% from 33.10% to 28.75% this week. Friday was very volatile, and I believe that the portfolio held up well. I have structured this account to focus on generating recurring income while mitigating downside risk rather than focusing on capital appreciation. I have been adding $100 per week and expanded the portfolio to $31,027.83 from $24,100 of invested capital and it is generating $2,649.70 in recurring dividend income. As time progress I am expecting the account to continue trending up and to the right in all categories and the $3,000 of generated income is going to be reached much sooner than I expected.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Dividend Section

Here's how much dividend income is generated per investment basket:

  • ETFs $951.35 (35.90%)
  • Equities $591.89 (22.34%)
  • CEFs $427.73 (16.14%)
  • REITs $421.14 (15.89%)
  • BDCs $257.59 (9.72%)

Investors utilize dividends to supplement their income and live off them. I'm building a dividend portfolio for myself 30 years into the future. In 2022, I collected $507.80 in dividend income from 533 dividends. In 2023, I collected $978.11 in dividend income from 660 dividends. 2024 was a great year, as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $1,623.14 in dividend income from 709 dividends. After 41 weeks in 2025, $1,822.31 of dividend income was produced, which is 112.27% of the total income from 2024. There were 616 positions that paid a dividend, which is 86.88% of the dividends generated in 2024.

There was $36.68 of dividend income generated this week, which brought the average amount of dividend income I have generated on a weekly basis to $44.45. This is an increase of $13.23 (42.39%) YoY, and there have been 4 weeks that exceeded $100, unlike in 2024, when the largest week was $89.13. I’m really excited for the rest of September to unfold, as the YoY data is showing me a huge trend going up and to the right with dividend income. Over the next several years, I should be able to exceed $5,000 in forward dividend income in this account and basically double my annualized allocation to the portfolio.

October is getting off to a strong start, as there has been $81.77 of income generated so far. In the 1st month of each quarter so far, $202.66, $191.22, and $209.73 of income have been produced. When I look at how the chart has developed, I believe that the days of generating less than $200 in monthly income are in the rear-view mirror. There are 3 full weeks left in October, and I believe this is going to be the largest month of generated income, considering it was the largest month back in 2024, It’s going to be exciting to see when the next milestones of generating $225 and $250 of income occur.

My long-term goal is to have 75% or more of the portfolio generate at least 1 share per year from their dividend income. There are currently 45 positions accomplishing this, which is emphasized by the green section of the table below. There is $545.91 of forward dividend income being generated from the new dividends being generated from all the positions generating at least 1 share from their dividends annually. Many positions are still lagging, but I am making a strong effort to allocate capital toward them so I can get at least 50% of the portfolio to accomplish this goal by the end of the year. My fixation on this is because it will allow me to advance the compounding impacts from the dividend snowball, and it also makes sure that I am not investing in the same positions each week.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Composition

ETFs are exceeding 20% of the portfolio again, and it's entirely my fault. I was also adding to REITs heading into the rate cut, which caused the portfolio to get a bit top-heavy. It’s difficult for me to stay away from these sectors, as I believe that technology is going to heat up throughout earnings season and that there are still a lot of deals in the REIT segment. This week I added to CEFs but ETFs are still 20.6% of the portfolio. I am going to work and get this down in the future. Individual equities now represent 39.17% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio while generating 22.39% of the dividend income. REITs, ETFs, CEFs, and BDCs make up 60.83% of the portfolio and generate 77.61% of the forward income. I am working on getting individual equities to represent more of the portfolio. The long-term goal is to have the sectors be close to equal weight, but that may not happen for 5–10 years at this rate, if ever. I have been adding energy companies as I believe there is a big opportunity there, and I will probably continue allocating capital toward energy positions as time progresses.

Just when Altria Group (MO) is starting to represent less of the portfolio, the market tanks, and good old MO doesn’t skip a beat. This is a classic definition of a defensive stock. I am quite happy that the top 20 positions are rounding out a bit further. While all my positions are under the 5% threshold, I really want MO to represent less of the portfolio, considering I haven’t added new shares to the position in over a year and a half. One of the keys to this portfolio’s success has been the level of diversification. Eventually, the top 3 positions will get smaller, and I will feel more comfortable allocating capital toward them. I am expecting the top 20 positions to change a bit next year, and I am hoping that I can get all the positions a bit more even in the future. While I have allowed myself a 5% maximum, I really want to have a well-rounded portfolio that can mitigate risk well.

This week the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) found its way back onto the list as Pfizer (PFE) fell out of the top-10 holdings. My allocation to the top 10 positions is $6,685.60, and they finished the week with a value of $9,095.35. This is an ROI of $2,409.74 (36.04%). There has been $1,367.03 of dividends generated and reinvested, which is 20.45% of the initial investment into these positions. I'm now projecting that $727.27 of dividend income will be generated from the top 10 holdings, which have a forward yield of 10.88%. These positions represent 29.31% of the portfolio, while their forward dividend income is projected to generate 27.45% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's annualized income.

Week 241 Additions

This week I added the Adams Diversified Equity Fund (ADX) to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio.

Adams Diversified Equity Fund

  • I was very interested in adding ADX to the portfolio because at the time it was trading at a -9.3% discount to NAV while generating an 8.18% yield
  • ADX has provided distributions for more than 85 years and has been public since 1929
  • ADX’s portfolio has stood the test of time as it's been able to evolve throughout every major transition period over the past decade
  • I wanted to own ADX because I was able to buy a great portfolio of assets at a steep discount and get paid a large distribution to do so

Week 242 gameplan

Next week I will probably add to assets that got hit hard on Friday, such as Starwood Property Trust (STWD) and Verizon (VZ).

Conclusion

October is starting out strong, and I believe it has the potential to be the largest month of distributed income in the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio’s history. When I look at the charts below the S-curve is forming and the recurring income makes weeks like this much easier to digest. I am excited for earnings season and believe the portfolio is positioned well to reach new heights in profitability and generated income. I think that there is a chance that I will generate between $650 and $700 of income in Q4 and set the stage for producing more than $3,000 of income in 2026. In times like this when geopolitical events impact the market I like to remind myself that ultimately the market is forward looking and has rebounded from every economic and geopolitical event and the current headwinds will also pass. I am excited to add to positions that I want to add more to at a better price. Please leave all your comments and suggestions in the comment section below as I try to interact with everyone throughout the week.

data.title: Nvidia: It's Not A Bubble, It's A Dam
data.description:
Since my last Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) analysis, in which I issued a Strong Buy rating, the stock has gained 12.4% in price.

Headlines are increasingly surfacing that suggest that we may be in an "AI bubble," however it doesn't take a genius to figure out that there is no bubble. Instead, we exist in a new age of AI. Indeed, this is the dawn of AI, of robotics, of autonomy, and perhaps eventually the end of human labor as we know it. But before we get ahead of ourselves, we need to recognize that there are significant caps to the notion of "infinite scaling" of artificial intelligence. Namely, the supply of energy and materials. More definitively, as the supply goes down for AI compute relative to demand, the price is going to go up, and customers will not be able to keep pace with the unsustainable ROI. So logically, America has a big problem on its hands: where is it going to get all of its cheap energy from, and how is it going to do it fast enough so AI prices don't cap adoption? These are questions I will let you ponder on your own accord, and they are questions I have already begun answering and allocating capital based on in private. In this analysis, we'll look at the dynamics of why these material constraints could lead to a temporary, though major, Nvidia valuation contraction before the next AI bull market resumes. I admit, this outcome is far from certain, but it's a meaningful non-tail risk that deserves defensive posturing if you play to thrive and survive.

The Bubble Hallucination

AI is a real adoption cycle. It's amusing when people who don't know the first thing about tech start to look at an infra cycle and say, "There's a bubble!" Have you ever traded semiconductors before AI? If you have, you'll know it looks like this:

This time is different. We're in a supercycle: a new AI age. However, even super-cycles have periods of high demand and pauses of low demand, unless there are no bottlenecks and the price of adoption is so low that enterprise and commercial customers can buy indefinitely without a bad effect on their income statements. In reality, the U.S. has finite energy capacity and limitations to certain critical resources, like rare earths, needed for AI development.

Big Tech companies have guided for about $400B combined in data center capex for 2025. But this relies heavily on the bottom-up demand of the cloud services customers who are funding the AI boom. Moreover, enterprises rely on their consumers (that's you, reader) to fund their AI tools. In essence, if users reach a point of "AI saturation," which would be akin to the feeling, "I already have AI; I don't need more," don't expect them to shell out more and more money for AI services. That means that enterprises will request fewer AI cloud services, which means that Big Tech companies will request fewer GPUs from Nvidia, Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), and others.

Please note that the following chart reflects the bear case only. There may be other speculative "doomsday" bear cases, but I do not consider them here because they are improbable.

Here's the data to substantiate my thesis. U.S. electricity demand is re-accelerating after a decade of flat growth, with the EIA projecting record consumption in 2025-2026, with data centers as a key driver. A recent Congressional Research Service brief also estimates that U.S. data centers used ~176 TWh in 2023, which constitutes ~4.4% of U.S. electricity. This could double or triple by 2028, up to around 12% of U.S. load, straining regional grids even if national totals look manageable. PJM, the largest U.S. grid, now forecasts ~3% annual summer peak growth for a decade, with net energy load up ~4.8% per year. Moreover, interconnection queues are slow, with LBNL showing wait times of years from request to operation, meaning near-term demand windows can be missed. One of the biggest chokepoints is grid hardware, where the DOE's Large Power Transformer supply chain report is revealing constrained, long-lead transformer capacity.

Let's put this into perspective. An 8-GPU DGX B200 system draws in around 14.3 kW at max capacity. 10,000 nodes is about 143 MW; 100,000 nodes is about 1.43 GW. On the upper end, that's one or more utility-scale plants for a single cluster family. It can't scale forever without infinite energy, which we currently do not have. Hence my thesis: the endless AI boom could evolve in phases, or cycles. For the uninitiated, "bubbles," but there really are no bubbles unless you want to count common valuation froth.

Then, we have to look at the materials layer. Even now, Nvidia's 10-K is explicit in that supply assurance requires prepaid manufacturing and capacity agreements, which remains incredibly challenging given the multi-supplier complexity of the AI chain. High-bandwidth memory is already sold out through 2025/2026 at Micron (MU), and TSMC (TSM) is expanding advanced packaging capacity across its sites because demand is so heavy.

However, the drawdown thesis could be falsified for a few reasons. Firstly, sustained efficiency leaps could keep cluster footprints inside existing substation limits; this is essentially getting "more with the same power." Then, grid build-outs could also accelerate, and more power could become available through U.S. alliances internationally. However, there are physical limitations on how fast the grid can be expanded, but our access to energy infrastructure quickly and at scale is the most reliable route to long-term AI-scaling sustainability. I am sure the U.S. government's Department of Energy is working on it. Higher capex from memory players and TSMC ramps will also mean that HBM and CoWoS throughput could stay stable and liquid. When there is demand, industry typically finds a way. That is why even in the bear case I see the AI thesis as being intact, just facing a momentary "pause" while infra catches up.

Valuation

All things considered, Nvidia is still cheap on a fundamental basis. The company's forward P/E non-GAAP ratio is 42, which is 66% higher than the sector median. Forward diluted EPS growth is 70%, which is 536% higher than the sector median.

I would like to reiterate that I do not think a cliff edge is necessarily going to occur. From a macro perspective, I think we are in an endless AI boom. This is not like the internet, which was essentially the invention of the visual phone. This is artificial intelligence, really a technology that marks the dawn of a whole new age, likely economically as well as technologically. That is why infinite scaling here applies, and Nvidia will likely continue to be the most valuable company in the world for decades to come, even if it faces periods of momentary infrastructure digestion and valuation correction (cycles of dominance). The company has positioned itself as the most advanced semiconductor designer in the world, paving the way for being the dependency mechanism of the AI revolution and likely the dependency mechanism of the robotics-at-scale revolution to come.

From a sentiment perspective, Nvidia is fairly valued right now. The stock price is trading along the 50-day moving average with the 14-day RSI at about 50. This is an opportune entry point as long as you maintain the composure that we are entering "risk-on territory."

Conclusion: Buy

I can't logically see how one would view Nvidia and say there is an AI bubble related to this company. There may be an AI capex slowdown, and that would logically lead to a stark valuation contraction. So the lesson here is don't have all your eggs in the AI basket. It might be okay for Huang and Lisa Su because they understand that they will have to ride volatility toward progressively new highs, but for fund managers and individual investors, it is probably not wise to be so exposed to that much downside risk from cyclical bear-case dynamics. That said, even in the heavy near-term contraction situation, this would present a buying opportunity for long-term alpha-producing CAGRs in the years to come. I'm staying bullish here but structuring my portfolio for resiliency given the energy and materials bottlenecks that are emerging.

data.title: The Next REITs That Could Potentially Cut Dividends (My Predictions)
data.description:
Dividend hikes are a strong catalyst that commonly leads to upside in the REIT sector (VNQ). But dividend cuts are arguably an even stronger catalyst the other way around.

Whenever a REIT cuts its dividend, the first reaction of investors is typically to sell and ask questions later.

That's because most REIT investors are income-oriented, and since everyone expects the stock to drop when a cut is announced, it then becomes a self-reinforcing prophecy as everyone tries to get out ahead of others.

For this reason, it's always crucial for investors to assess the dividend sustainability of a REIT.

Unfortunately, there are quite a few REITs that have too much leverage and have set their payout ratios too high, which puts them at high risk of a future cut.

In what follows, I will highlight three REITs that we fear will eventually have to cut their dividend. We monitor them closely because we would gladly initiate positions if and when they cut in the future and their share prices crash as a result of it.

CTO Realty Growth (CTO)

CTO is a very popular REIT among individual investors, and that's because it's today offering a 9% dividend yield.

Moreover, this dividend appears to be fully covered with an 82% FFO payout ratio, which leads many to think that the dividend is sustainable.

It also owns desirable assets, mostly service-oriented strip centers in sunbelt markets, which are today enjoying growing rents.

Many of its properties are also leased at below-market rents, which positions it for strong continued rent growth over the coming years.

So there's a lot to like about the REIT.

But here's the issue. Its payout ratio adjusted for capex and leasing cost is very tight at near 100%.

Moreover, the company's debt-to-EBITDA is at nearly 7x, and it still has additional preferred equity on top of that, adding even more leverage.

And despite enjoying strong property-sector fundamentals, its same property NOI is only expected to grow by about 1% this year due to a few tenant bankruptcies.

In the last quarter earnings call, they did not mention the dividend once, and I think that this is ultimately because they're unsure about its sustainability as well.

They may try to delay the cut for a while longer, but I think that a cut is very likely later this year or in 2026.

Gladstone Commercial (GOOD)

I'm less certain about GOOD, but once again, its payout ratio is high relative to the leverage of the company and the risk profile of its assets.

The current FFO payout ratio is high but still reasonable at 85%.

But the problem is that its debt-to-EBITDA is at nearly 7x, and again, it also has additional preferred equity on top of that, adding even more leverage. It also has over half of its debt maturing in 2026 and 2027:

Moreover, it has historically bought mainly higher cap rate industrial net lease properties with cap rates of 8-9%. You don't get such high cap rates without taking some risks, and I fear that GOOD could deal with growing vacancies as a result of a recession and/or the impact of the trade war.

But what worries me even more is its office properties, which still generate nearly one-third of its rental revenue. Single-tenant net lease office REITs like Orion Properties (ONL) have seen their share prices crash 80%-plus over the past years because the outlook for this asset class is so challenging. They're retaining nearly all their cash flow to prepare for costly vacancies, and I think that GOOD should prepare as well.

To give the management credit where it's due, they have done a great job at selling some of their offices to reinvest in better industrial properties. Unfortunately, the exposure remains significant, and the outlook for those properties will only get worse with the AI revolution disrupting white-collar labor markets and likely further reducing the demand for such large single-tenant office buildings.

I think that this will eventually end in a dividend cut.

They have cut in the past, and I think that they will need to cut again.

With that said, we like the preferred equity of the company (GOODO) and think that its dividend is sustainable.

One Liberty Properties (OLP)

OLP is a similar story.

They have greatly increased their exposure to industrial properties in recent years, which is great on paper, but a lot of those properties were riskier assets bought at high cap rates.

They're great as long as you have years left on your leases and the economy is growing, but as lease expirations approach and/or the environment gets worse, they could face growing issues. At the very least, we expect growing capex needs as leases expire to retain tenants, and with the average remaining lease term at just 4.5 years, this is an important topic to consider.

Even then, the AFFO payout ratio is already high at about 94%. If they had low leverage and their assets were expected to consistently grow cash flow with limited capex needs, this could be fine, but this is not the case.

The REIT has a 6.4x debt-to-EBITDA, which is also higher than average.

Therefore, I think that this REIT will also likely eventually need to cut its dividend.

Closing Note

All three of these REITs could eventually become great investment opportunities.

There's a lot to like about all three of them.

But at High Yield Landlord, we will wait for them to first cut their dividend, and only then will we consider investing in them.

data.title: Modest U.K. Growth Rebound Won't Ease Chancellor's Autumn Budget Challenge
data.description:
By James Smith

The UK economy grew by 0.1% in August, offsetting a fall in July, driven partly by a volatile couple of months for manufacturing. It keeps the UK economy on track to grow by roughly 0.2% in the third quarter overall.

We’re always reticent to read too much into these volatile monthly figures, which have been even more bumpy over recent months, given the frontloading ahead of tariffs and stamp duty changes earlier in the year. Generally, though, the economy is performing a little better than expected a few months ago, helped by upward revisions to growth towards the end of last year. That helped boost our annual GDP forecast to 1.5% for this year.

Some of that strength can be traced back to the material rise in government spending we've seen this year. Real-terms departmental budgets are rising by 4%, having risen by almost 3% in the last fiscal year. Output in government-related sectors has been growing more quickly in annual terms than the wider economy. The effects are even more apparent in the jobs market, where rising government hiring has offset steady falls in private sector employment.

That tailwind is likely to diminish next year. In our view, the combination of downgrades to the OBR’s economic forecasts (chiefly productivity), higher gilt yields and policy U-turns leaves a £25bn a year budget hole, relative to the situation at the March Spring Statement. Despite political pressure, that likely means the government will be unable to materially deviate from its previous spending plans, which sees departmental budgets growing much less rapidly in the next fiscal year. Set that against a significant increase in taxes, and we think government policy following the Autumn Budget will pose more of a headwind to growth in 2026.

Content Disclaimer

data.title: FX Moves Tentatively, While Equities And Gold Continue To March Higher
data.description:
Overview

The US dollar is mostly a little softer ahead of the start of the North American session. Participants do not seem to have much short-term conviction amid the heightened tensions between the US and China and wary of political developments in France and Japan. Against several pairs, the greenback has frayed its recent ranges, but there has been limited follow-through. Poor Australian jobs data boosted market speculation of a rate cut next month, while a recovery in the UK's August GDP is helping lift sterling to the top of the G10 currency performers today. French Prime Minister Lecornu has survived one of two no-confidence motions today after yesterday's significant compromises bought the government some time. Unexpectedly aggressive intervention by the Reserve Bank of India yesterday and US claims that India has agreed to stop buying Russian oil have seen further gains in the Indian rupee today, making it among the strongest of the emerging market currencies today.

Equities continue to march higher. Led by a 2.5% rally in South Korea's Kospi and a 1.3% rise in Taiwan's Taiex, most equity markets in the Asia-Pacific region advanced today. Europe's Stoxx 600 (STOXX) is about 0.40% higher, and, if sustained, it would be the third gain this week. US index futures are 0.30%-0.45% better. The disappointing Australian jobs report sent its 10-year yield down more than six basis points (to around 4.14%), while European benchmark 10-year yields are narrowly mixed. The 10-year Treasury yield continues to hover around 4.0%, now 4.02%. Gold stretched to a new record (~$4242) but is now around $4230. December WTI is holding above $58 today but below $58.65.

USD: The Dollar Index (DXY) has finally broken out of last Thursday's range (~98.70-99.55) to the downside, falling to almost 98.40 today. This overshot the (50%) retracement of this month's gains. The 20-day moving average and the (61.8%) retracement are in the 98.20-25 area. The government remains closed, and some two million federal employees will go without a paycheck this week. A federal judge ruled yesterday against any more "reduction in force lay-offs". Still, both political parties appear to be retrenching rather than seeking a compromise. Even with the government shut, the economic calendar is not empty. Today features three surveys: the October Philadelphia Fed survey, the NY Fed's business services survey, and NAHB home builders survey. Still, when everything is said and done, the derivatives markets show near full confidence of a rate cut later this month and in December.

Euro: The euro appears to have forged a base around $1.1540 but has yet to prove itself on the upside. Yesterday, it held below last Thursday's high, slightly below $1.1650, and the (50%) retracement of this month's decline is around $1.1660. Today it has been bid to $1.1675. The next retracement (61.8%) and the 20-day moving average are around $1.1690. France's immediate political logjam has broken with significant and necessary compromises by President Macron. Prime Minister Lecornu has survived one of two no-confidence votes today, but Macron's reform agenda has been stymied, and Lecornu has promised not to use the constitutional provision (Article 49.3) that allows bypassing the National Assembly, which minority French governments have relied on.

CNY: The US dollar has frayed the lower end of the range that has confined it since last Thursday against the offshore yuan (~CNH7.1240-CNH7.1500). It was sold to almost CNH7.1210 today before recovering to almost CNH7.1315. The PBoC lowered the dollar's reference rate to its lowest level in nearly a year. It was set at CNY7.0995 yesterday and CNY7.0968 today. Meanwhile, the Dutch government's actions (to take control of Chinese-owned subsidiary of Wingtech, Nexperia) continue to be discussed. In late September, the US had put 100s of subsidiaries of Chinese sanctioned companies on its entity list (which appears to be part of the context of Beijing's tightening and broadening of its critical minerals regime), and it had reportedly threatened to include Nexperia, according to press reports. The Dutch government quickly capitulated but did not go public until last week. Beijing responded this week by blocking Nexperia China and its subcontractors from exporting some components, which appears to undermine the Dutch operations.

JPY: The dollar peaked at the end of last week around JPY153.25. Yesterday's low was near JPY150.90, and today it has been sold to JPY150.50. Options for almost $830 mln expire at JPY150.50 today. The (38.2%) retracement of this month's rally was near JPY150.70, and the (50%) retracement is slightly below JPY150.00. The exchange rate seems caught between the soft US yields on the one hand and Japanese politics on the other. The new head of the LDP wants easy monetary and fiscal policy, and she looks poised to be the next prime minister. However, her inability to find a compromise with her junior ally for 26 years, the Komeito Party, and the severing of the alliance has created an opportunity for the main opposition parties. Three opposition parties are exploring the scope of an agreement to support the head of the Democratic Party for the People (DPP), Tamaki, as their prime minister candidate. Meanwhile, Takaichi has reached out to the Osaka-based opposition party Japan Innovation Party (Ishin) for support for her bid to be prime minister. The DPP and the Constitutional Democratic Party are also trying to woo Ishin. The vote is next week. August was a rough month for Japan. Yesterday, it revised down the month's industrial output to -1.5% from -1.2%, and today it reported a 0.4% decline in the tertiary industry index (from a revised 0.2% gain in July, which was initially reported at 0.5%). The enthusiasm for a rate hike this month peaked at the end of September with about a 70% chance of a hike, the most since April. Now, with a new LDP head who could still very well be the next prime minister advocating easy monetary and fiscal policy and poor economic data in tow, the swaps market has around a 15% chance of a hike priced.

GBP: Sterling reached a four-day high slightly above $1.3400 yesterday and extended the recovery today to almost $1.3445. Recall that it set a two-and-a-half-month low on Tuesday near $1.3250. Near today's high, sterling met the (38.2%) of the decline since the FOMC rate cut a month ago. The (50%) retracement is slightly above $1.3485. The UK reported that the economy expanded by 0.1% in August after July's unchanged estimate was revised to a 0.1% contraction. Industrial output rose 0.4% (-0.9% in July was revised to -0.4%), while the index of services activity was flat and July's 0.1% gain was revised away. The trade balance deteriorated slightly, while construction output weakened (-0.3% after a 0.2% gain in July was revised to zero).

CAD: After reaching its best level in six months on Tuesday near CAD1.4080, the greenback consolidated yesterday. Previous resistance around CAD1.4020 now offers support. The daily momentum indicators are stretched but have not turned down. The greenback may need to take out the CAD1.3965 area to begin signaling a top. Nearby resistance is around CAD1.4100, though the CAD1.4165 area is the (50%) retracement of this year's decline. Economic data shows the Canadian economy softened in August, but the employment data from last week provides some sense of optimism that it performed better last month.

AUD: The Australian dollar managed to extend Tuesday's recovery. It reached $0.6440 on Tuesday, its lowest level in nearly two months, before recovering to almost $0.6525 in North America. A disappointing jobs report pushed the Aussie to $0.6480 before recovering back above $0.6500. Options for about A$435 mln at $0.6500 expire today. Overall employment rose by almost 15k (the 5.4k loss in August was doubled in the revision to -11.9k). Full-time posts increased by almost 9k after falling by a revised 48.6k in August (from -40.9k initially). Labor supply growth was almost due to an increase in new employment, and this saw the unemployment rate rise to 4.5% from a revised 4.3% (from 4.2% initially). The odds of a rate cut at next month's meeting, as reflected in the indicative pricing in the futures market, have risen to about 67% from about 36% yesterday. It was near 31% at the end of last month.

MXN: The dollar remains confined to the range set at the end of last week against the Mexican peso of roughly MXN18.3635-MXN18.6370. While yesterday's low was near MXN18.4350, the MXN18.42 area is the middle of the range that has prevailed since the FOMC meeting on September 17 to cut rates. The greenback is trading between about MXN18.4350 to MXN18.4815 so far today. Options for almost $400 mln at MXN18.39 expire today. Mexico's economic calendar picks up next week with the IGAE report, which is similar to a monthly GDP, August retail sales, and inflation for the first half of October. The central bank meets again on November 6. Pending more data, we suspect that Banxico may pause but signal that the easing cycle is not over.
